title: Anesthesia for Disorders of Blood MCQ – Chapter 26
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What is the most common type of anemia encountered in clinical practice in India?

options.B: Microcytic hypochromic anemia due to iron deficiency

questions.explanation: The most common anemia encountered in clinical practice in India is microcytic hypochromic due to iron deficiency.

questions.question: What is the minimum hemoglobin level considered optimal to accept a patient for elective surgery?
questions.explanation: A minimum hemoglobin of 8 g/dL is considered optimal to accept the patient for elective surgery.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating maximum allowable blood loss?

options.A: (Patient Hct - Desired Hct) / Desired Hct x Blood volume
options.B: (Patient Hct - Desired Hct) / Patient Hct x Blood volume
options.C: Patient Hct / Desired Hct x Blood volume
options.D: (Desired Hct - Patient Hct) / Patient Hct x Blood volume

questions.explanation: Maximum allowable blood loss = (Hematocrit of patient - Desirable hematocrit) / Hematocrit of patient x Blood volume.

questions.question: What is the estimated blood volume used in the calculation for allowable blood loss?
questions.explanation: Blood volume of 80-90 mL/kg is used in the calculation for maximum allowable blood loss.

questions.question: What is the current day approach towards minimum use of blood products called?
questions.explanation: The approach in present day practice is towards the minimum use of blood products called restrictive approach to transfusion.

questions.question: When is blood transfusion generally indicated in a normal patient?

options.B: Blood loss > 20% of blood volume or Hb < 8 g%

questions.explanation: Blood loss greater than 20% of blood volume or hemoglobin level less than 8 g% in a normal patient indicates transfusion.

questions.question: At what hemoglobin level is transfusion indicated in a patient with ischemic heart disease?
questions.explanation: In a patient with major disease like ischemic heart disease, transfusion is indicated when Hb is less than 10 g%.

questions.question: What type of anesthesia should be avoided for vitamin B12 deficiency anemia?
questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia should be avoided for vitamin B12 deficiency anemia due to possibility of neurological deficits.

questions.question: Which inhalational agent should be avoided in megaloblastic anemia?
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide can cause megaloblastic anemia therefore should be avoided for vitamin B12 deficiency anemia.

questions.question: What effect does alkalosis have on the oxygen dissociation curve?
questions.explanation: Alkalosis (especially caused by hyperventilation) shifts the oxygen dissociation curve to the left, increasing the oxygen requirement.

questions.question: How is the uptake of inhalational agents affected in anemia patients?

options.B: Increased uptake offset by increased cardiac output

questions.explanation: Increase uptake of inhalational agents is offset by increased cardiac output seen in anemia patients.

questions.question: What is the risk status of patients having sickle cell trait?

options.B: No increase in morbidity and mortality

questions.explanation: There is no increase in morbidity and mortality in patients having sickle cell trait while patients with sickle cell disease are at high risk.

questions.question: Which patients with sickle cell disease should NOT be taken for elective surgery?
questions.explanation: Patients in acute crisis phase must not be taken for elective surgery.

questions.question: What is the target hematocrit for red cell transfusion in sickle cell patients?
questions.explanation: The aim is to correct the anemia by giving red cell transfusion with the target hematocrit > 30 (Hemoglobin > 10 g%).

questions.question: What is the most important perioperative goal in sickle cell patients?
questions.explanation: The most important perioperative goal is to avoid factors which can precipitate sickling: hypoxia, acidosis, dehydration, hypothermia, stress and pain.

questions.question: Which factors can precipitate sickling in sickle cell disease?

options.B: Hypoxia, acidosis, dehydration, hypothermia, stress and pain

questions.explanation: Factors that precipitate sickling include hypoxia, acidosis, dehydration, hypothermia, stress and pain.

questions.question: How should dehydration be managed preoperatively in sickle cell patients?

options.B: IV fluids should be started in preoperative period

questions.explanation: To avoid dehydration, intravenous fluids should be started in preoperative period.

questions.question: What premedication is recommended for sickle cell patients to avoid stress and anxiety?
questions.explanation: To avoid stress and anxiety these patients must be premedicated with benzodiazepines.

questions.question: Is intravenous regional anesthesia (Bier's block) safe in sickle cell patients?

options.A: Yes, it is the preferred technique
options.B: No, it does not hold true in current day practice due to tourniquet use

questions.explanation: The old recommendation of giving IV regional anesthesia (Bier's block) to sickle cell patients does not hold true in current day practice due to tourniquet use.

questions.question: Can tourniquet be used in sickle cell patients?

options.B: Can be used safely with minimal possible pressure for minimal period
options.C: Can be used without any precautions

questions.explanation: Per current guidelines, tourniquet can be used safely in sickle cell patients with a little higher complications. It is recommended to use for minimal period with minimal possible pressure.

questions.question: What must be avoided at any cost in sickle cell patients?
questions.explanation: The factors which precipitate sickling must be avoided at any cost.

questions.question: When does acute lung syndrome usually occur postoperatively in sickle cell patients?
questions.explanation: Acute lung syndrome usually occurs after 48-72 hours in postoperative period.

questions.question: What is the risk level of patients with thalassemia minor?
questions.explanation: Patients with thalassemia minor are not high risk while those with thalassemia major are high risk patients for perioperative morbidity and mortality.

questions.question: What can skeletal abnormalities of maxilla in thalassemia cause?
questions.explanation: Skeletal abnormalities of maxilla (maxillary overgrowth) can make intubation difficult.

questions.question: What organ involvement should be assessed in thalassemia major patients?

options.B: Hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal involvement, CHF, and consequences of iron overload including cirrhosis

questions.explanation: It is important to assess organ involvement like hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal involvement, congestive heart failure and consequences of iron overload like cirrhosis.

questions.question: What is the target hematocrit for polycythemia patients before elective surgery?
questions.explanation: Reduce hematocrit to <45 and platelet count to less than 4 lakhs before taking the patient for elective surgery.

questions.question: What additional bleeding risk exists in polycythemia patients?
questions.explanation: Patients with polycythemia are at increased risk of bleeding due to decrease von Willebrand factor seen in polycythemia.

questions.question: What is the recommended platelet count for considering surgery in thrombocytopenia?
questions.explanation: Platelet count of > 50,000/mm3 is recommended before considering for surgery.

questions.question: At what platelet count can neurosurgical patients be considered for surgery?
questions.explanation: Neurosurgical patients cannot be considered for surgery if their platelet count is < 100,000.

questions.question: Which patients need platelet transfusion before surgery regardless of platelet count?
questions.explanation: Symptomatic patients (spontaneous bleeding) irrespective of their platelet count need platelet transfusion before surgery.

questions.question: Above what platelet count can central neuraxial blocks be safely given?
questions.explanation: Patients with platelet count more than 80,000/cubic mm can be safely given central neuraxial blocks.

questions.question: What is the relative contraindication range for platelet count regarding central neuraxial blocks?
questions.explanation: Platelet count between 50,000 and 80,000/cubic mm is a relative contraindication while count less than 50,000 is an absolute contraindication.

questions.question: What should the deficit coagulation factor be increased to before considering a patient for surgery?
questions.explanation: The deficit factor should be increased to at least 50% of the normal before considering the patient for surgery.

questions.question: How can coagulation factors be replenished preoperatively?

options.B: Fresh frozen plasma, cryoprecipitate or recombinant factor

questions.explanation: The deficit factor can be increased by administration of fresh frozen plasma, cryoprecipitate or recombinant factor.

questions.question: What type of anesthesia is contraindicated in coagulation deficiency disorders?
questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia is contraindicated in coagulation deficiency disorders.

questions.question: Why should intramuscular route be avoided in coagulation disorders?
questions.explanation: Intramuscular route should be avoided in coagulation disorders due to risk of bleeding/hematoma.

questions.question: Who should perform intubation in patients with massive bleeding in oral cavity from coagulation disorders?
questions.explanation: Intubation should be performed by expert hand with minimum trauma, otherwise there can be massive bleeding in oral cavity.

questions.question: For how long should antithrombin levels be maintained > 80% postoperatively in hypercoagulable disorders?
questions.explanation: Antithrombin levels should be > 80% at least for 5 days in postoperative period.

questions.question: Why are central neuraxial blocks preferred in hypercoagulable disorders?

options.B: They decrease incidence of thromboembolic complications

questions.explanation: Central neuraxial blocks are preferred because they decrease the incidence of thromboembolic complications (due to increase blood in lower limb and pelvic veins).

questions.question: When should DVT prophylaxis be started in hypercoagulable disorders?
questions.explanation: All prophylaxis against deep venous thrombosis (DVT) should be started as early as possible.

questions.question: What does the anesthetic management of G6PD deficiency patients include?

options.B: Assessing severity of anemia and avoiding drugs that trigger hemolysis

questions.explanation: The anesthetic management includes assessing the severity of anemia and avoiding drugs and conditions which can trigger hemolysis in G6PD deficient individuals.

questions.question: Which benzodiazepines are safe in G6PD deficiency?
questions.explanation: Diazepam; interestingly, midazolam is safe in G6PD deficiency.

questions.question: Which inhalational agents can trigger hemolysis in G6PD deficiency?
questions.explanation: Isoflurane and sevoflurane can trigger hemolysis in perioperative period in G6PD deficient patients.

questions.question: Which local anesthetics should be avoided in G6PD deficiency?
questions.explanation: Prilocaine, benzocaine, lignocaine and nitroglycerine should be avoided as they can cause methemoglobinemia which can cause significant hemolysis.

questions.question: What is the danger of administering methylene blue to G6PD deficient patients?
questions.explanation: Administrating methylene blue to these patients can be life threatening.

questions.question: What conditions can trigger hemolysis in G6PD deficiency besides drugs?
questions.explanation: Acidosis, hypothermia, hyperglycemia and infection can trigger hemolysis and must be avoided throughout in these patients.

questions.question: Which drugs are absolutely contraindicated in porphyria?
questions.explanation: Barbiturates (Thiopentone/Methohexitone) are absolutely contraindicated as they induce aminolevulinic acid synthetase and precipitate all kinds of porphyrias except porphyria cutanea tarda and erythropoietic protoporphyrias.

questions.question: Which agents are considered safe in porphyria patients?
questions.explanation: Propofol, opioids, suxamethonium are considered as safe agents in porphyria.

questions.question: Should regional anesthesia be used in porphyria patients with peripheral neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia should be avoided in the porphyria patients with peripheral neuropathy.

questions.question: What can fasting, dehydration and infection trigger in porphyria patients?
questions.explanation: Fasting, dehydration and infection can trigger acute crises in porphyria patients.
